questions.prompt: Which of these statements is the most accurate description of E-E-A-T?

choices.text: It's the single most important ranking factor in Google's algorithm.
It's a framework Google's quality raters use to evaluate content. It's not a direct ranking factor — the algorithm uses many separate signals that correlate with good E-E-A-T.
It only applies to medical and financial websites.
It's a score Google computes for every page in the index.

questions.explanation: Google explicitly says E-E-A-T 'isn't a specific ranking factor.' Quality raters use the E-E-A-T framework to evaluate whether Google's algorithm is producing good results. The algorithm then uses many independent signals — bylines, citations, link patterns, content accuracy — that collectively correlate with what raters would call good E-E-A-T.

questions.prompt: Of the four letters in E-E-A-T, which one does Google call the 'most important'?

choices.text: Experience — first-hand familiarity with the topic.
Trustworthiness — honesty, accuracy, transparency. The other three contribute to trust.

questions.explanation: Google's documentation states it plainly: 'Of these aspects, trust is most important. The others contribute to trust, but content doesn't necessarily have to demonstrate all of them.' A page can succeed with strong Experience or Expertise even without all four — but if it's not trustworthy, the rest doesn't save it.
